items.title: Tubal factor
items.contentHtml:
Tubal factor

  • It is the most common female cause of infertility, representing up to 30-40% of cases.
  • The pathophysiology includes obstruction, peritubal adhesions or ciliary dysfunction, often due to pelvic inflammatory disease.
  • hysterosalpingography is the first-line study to evaluate tubal patency; requires opacification of both tubes and peritoneal dispersion of the contrast.
  • A previous tubal ligation is a direct risk factor for infertility due to obstruction.
  • Chlamydia trachomatis is the most associated infection; causes asymptomatic tubal damage and fibrosis.

items.title: Hysterosalpingography
items.contentHtml:
Hysterosalpingography

  • First-line radiological procedure that uses iodinated contrast injected into the uterine cavity to visualize tubal patency.
  • Key finding: opacification of both fallopian tubes with free passage to the peritoneal cavity confirms patency.
  • Proximal obstruction suggests spasm or polyps; the distal one (hydrosalpinx) is associated with previous pelvic inflammatory disease.
  • It is performed in the early follicular phase (days 7-10 of the cycle) to avoid incipient pregnancy.
  • Contraindications: active pelvic infection, allergy to iodinated contrast, pregnancy.

items.foco: Previous tubal ligation as a factor
items.title: Previous tubal ligation
items.contentHtml:
Previous tubal ligation

  • Previous tubal ligation is a mechanical cause of infertility due to surgical obstruction of the tubes.
  • Surgical reversal (tubal anastomosis) has variable success (50-80%) depending on the technique and remaining segment.
  • Alternative: in vitro fertilization, which avoids the need for tubal patency.
  • The rate of ectopic pregnancy after reversal is higher (up to 5-10%) due to alterations in ciliary motility.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with hysterosalpingography that shows bilateral tubal obstruction.

items.title: Chlamydia trachomatis
items.contentHtml:
Chlamydia trachomatis infection

  • Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common bacterial sexually transmitted infection as a cause of tubal infertility.
  • Asymptomatic course in 70-80% of women; It produces silent pelvic inflammatory disease with fibrosis and tubal obstruction.
  • Diagnosis: detection of nucleic acid in cervical or first void samples.
  • Treatment: azithromycin 1 g single dose or doxycycline 100 mg every 12 hours for 7 days; treat a sexual partner simultaneously.
  • Tubal damage is irreversible, so prevention with screening in those under 25 years of age is key.

items.title: Varicocele
items.contentHtml:
Varicocele

  • It is the most common reversible cause of male infertility, present in 35-40% of infertile men.
  • Abnormal dilation of the pampiniform plexus due to testicular venous reflux, most common in the left testicle.
  • Pathophysiology: scrotal hyperthermia (>2°C) that alters spermatogenesis, with an increase in reactive species of oxygen.
  • Indication for varicocelectomy: palpable varicocele (grade II-III) with altered spermogram and infertility >1 year.
  • Surgery improves seminal parameters by 60-80% and spontaneous pregnancy rate by 30-50%.

items.foco: Cryptorchidism and its impact on fertility
items.title: Cryptorchidism
items.contentHtml:
Cryptorchidism

  • Cryptorchidism (testicle not descended into the scrotum) is the most common congenital genital anomaly in men (1-3% per year of life).
  • If it is not corrected before 12-18 months, it causes irreversible damage to germ cells due to hyperthermia.
  • The risk of infertility is proportional to the exposure time: Late orchidopexy (>2 years) significantly reduces sperm count.
  • It is associated with oligospermia and increased sperm DNA fragmentation rate.
  • Untreated bilateral cryptorchidism leads to azoospermia in 30-50% of cases; Unilateral has a better prognosis.

items.title: Spermogram
items.contentHtml:
Spermogram

  • Basal semen analysis according to World Health Organization criteria (6th edition, 2021).
  • Oligospermia: sperm concentration <16 million/mL.
  • Asthenospermia: progressive motility <30%.
  • Teratospermia: morphology normal <4% (strict Kruger criteria).
  • At least two samples with 2-7 days of abstinence are required to confirm alterations.
  • Combined oligosthenoteratospermia suggests multifactorial testicular damage; Normal ejaculate volume threshold: ≥1.4 mL.

items.title: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
items.contentHtml:
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism

  • Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is a cause of male infertility due to gonadotropin deficiency (follicle stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone).
  • It can be congenital (Kallmann syndrome: anosmia + hypogonadism) or acquired (pituitary tumors, hemochromatosis).
  • Diagnosis: low serum testosterone, low or inappropriately normal follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone.
  • Treatment: exogenous gonadotropins (recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone + human chorionic gonadotropin) to stimulate spermatogenesis.
  • Expected response: appearance of sperm in 6-18 months; 70-80% success rate to achieve spermatogenesis.

items.title: Drugs and spermatogenesis
items.contentHtml:
Drugs that affect spermatogenesis and anabolic steroids suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, reducing intratesticular testosterone.
  • Antihypertensives such as calcium channel blockers (nifedipine) are associated with reversible asthenospermia.
  • Others: sulfasalazine (oligospermia), cimetidine (receptor blockade androgenic), and finasteride (decrease in dihydrotestosterone and seminal volume).

items.title: Azoospermia
items.contentHtml:
Azoospermia: obstructive vs non-obstructive

  • azoospermia is the absence of sperm in the ejaculate; It is classified as obstructive and non-obstructive.
  • Obstructive azoospermia: normal spermatogenesis, but obstruction in the epididymis, vas deferens or ejaculator; causes: vasectomy, infections, cystic fibrosis (mutation of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator gene).
  • Non-obstructive azoospermia: primary testicular failure (hypospermatogenesis, arrested maturation, Sertoli cell syndrome) or secondary (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism).
  • Differential diagnosis: elevated follicle-stimulating hormone suggests non-obstructive; normal/low follicle stimulating hormone with normal testes suggests obstructive.
  • Treatment: obstructive → surgical reconstruction or testicular sperm extraction with in vitro fertilization-intracytoplasmic sperm injection; non-obstructive → testicular microsurgery for sperm extraction.

items.title: Sperm DNA fragmentation
items.contentHtml:
Sperm DNA fragmentation index

  • The sperm DNA fragmentation index measures the damage to the DNA of sperm; a threshold >30% is associated with a lower fertilization rate and a higher spontaneous abortion.
  • Evaluation methods: chromatin dispersion assay (Halosperm test), TUNEL or single cell gel electrophoresis (Comet).
  • Causes of high fragmentation: varicocele, genital infections, smoking, oxidative stress, advanced paternal age (>40 years).
  • A high index does not contraindicate in vitro fertilization, but may indicate the need for intracytoplasmic sperm injection with sperm selection (for example, binding to hyaluronic acid).
  • The reduction of the index is achieved with antioxidants (vitamin E, zinc, coenzyme Q10) and correction of underlying factors.

items.foco: IVF-ICSI indication in severe male factor
items.title: IVF-ICSI
items.contentHtml:
IVF-ICSI in severe male factor

  • In vitro fertilization with intracytoplasmic sperm injection is indicated in severe male factor: extreme oligospermia (<1 million/mL), obstructive or non-obstructive azoospermia with testicular sperm extraction, or severe teratospermia (<1% normal forms).
  • Procedure: direct injection of a sperm in the cytoplasm of the oocyte, avoiding the need for training and natural penetration.
  • In obstructive azoospermia, sperm are obtained by percutaneous aspiration of sperm from the epididymis or testicular sperm extraction; in non-obstructive, testicular microsurgery for sperm extraction.
  • Fertilization rate per cycle: 60-80%, regardless of semen quality, but the implantation rate depends on oocyte quality.
  • Risks: higher rate of chromosomal anomalies (transmission of Y chromosome microdeletions) and multiple births.
